html:
GBT is not the CCL/AI networking company in the prior draft. The real company is GreenBase Technology, a NEXCOM-linked Taiwan video AIoT micro-cap that designs and sells NDAA-compliant network cameras, NVRs, edge-AI video systems, and vertical-market surveillance solutions. That correction matters because the thesis is still interesting, just for a different reason: this is a tiny, illiquid, newly listed industrial vision supplier being priced as a post-debut surveillance hardware name, while the forward option is smart ports, public-safety infrastructure, transportation, and defense/drone vision.

At TWD 56.10, the market cap is about TWD 1.12 billion, or roughly USD 35 million. The stock has already round-tripped from the March 2026 Emerging Stock debut hype: reference price TWD 32, intraday high TWD 88, now down about 36% from that peak. Retail perception is therefore muddy. The first wave saw “NEXCOM little golden chicken, AI, defense” excitement, but the stock now screens like a thinly traded post-IPO hardware company with limited data coverage. That is the opportunity. The financials are not optically screaming cheap on stale trailing earnings, but they become cheap if the company returns even partway to its 2023 margin structure while adding AI/vertical-market project revenue.

The business has real substance. GreenBase says it is a strategic NEXCOM subsidiary, made in Taiwan, NDAA-compliant, and a supplier to tier-one global brands in video AIoT. Its product stack includes PTZ cameras, LPR/ANPR traffic cameras, mobile/rail cameras, desktop and rackmount NVRs, and customized AIoT video systems. The company’s own service positioning is end-to-end design, development, system build, production, and support, not just catalog camera resale. The most important product clue is the newer NVR line: 14th/13th Gen Intel Core processors, hybrid-cloud video architecture, PCIe expansion, and optional Hailo AI acceleration for edge analytics. That is where the stock’s label starts to shift from “camera ODM” to “industrial vision compute node.”

The official TPEX registration document shows 2025 revenue of TWD 648 million, up 26.9% from 2024, EPS of TWD 2.60, gross margin of 31.1%, operating margin of 9.3%, and 92.6% of revenue from digital security monitoring systems. That looks ordinary until you compare it with 2023: revenue was TWD 711 million, gross margin 37.5%, operating margin 18.0%, and EPS TWD 5.72. In other words, the upside does not require a fantasy software pivot. It only requires revenue scale and mix to recover toward a margin level the company has already produced.

The near-term revenue tape is mixed but improving. January-February 2026 was weak, which management attributed around the listing to Lunar New Year shipment timing and orders still to be delivered. March revenue was TWD 77.4 million, April was TWD 70.8 million, and April grew 50.8% year over year, bringing January-April revenue to TWD 222.9 million, down only 7.0% year over year after the early-year hole. If the April run-rate holds, annualized revenue is already around TWD 850 million. If project shipments for smart ports, public safety, and transportation hit in the second half, TWD 750-850 million of 2026 revenue is realistic rather than heroic.

The variant perception is that GreenBase is not trying to win consumer surveillance. It is positioned in enterprise and critical-infrastructure video, where Chinese supply-chain concerns and NDAA compliance matter. The company’s disclosed strategy splits into Smart Vision, meaning optical/mechanical/electrical integration and edge devices, and AI Vision, meaning recognition models and AI algorithms. The public customer proof point is Taiwan’s smart-port ecosystem: GreenBase-linked coverage cites subsidiary work with Evergreen at Kaohsiung’s Seventh Container Terminal, where OCR and AI vision helped reduce container handoff time from roughly 1-2 hours to about 10 minutes. Evergreen’s own terminal page confirms the broader setup: OCR, CCTV, electronic access control, IoT coverage, and automated gate/yard systems. That is a better reference customer than a generic security-camera channel.

The balance sheet gives time for the thesis to play out. At 2025 year-end, cash and equivalents were about TWD 229 million, while long-term debt was only TWD 17.7 million and current assets covered current liabilities by roughly 2.5x. Net cash is roughly TWD 10 per share before the proposed TWD 2.00 cash dividend. At today’s price, the dividend yield is about 3.6%. This is not a net-cash deep value story, because the stock still trades around 3.3x book and 21.6x trailing EPS. But the cash, dividend, and NEXCOM parentage reduce financing risk while the company pushes into larger vertical projects.

The right way to underwrite the stock is forward earnings power. In a flat hardware case, TWD 2.5-3.0 EPS is fair and the stock is not cheap. In the base case, revenue moves toward TWD 800-900 million over 2026-2027, gross margin recovers to 34-35%, and operating margin returns to the low-to-mid teens as R&D and project engineering are absorbed. That produces TWD 4.0-5.0 EPS. At 56.10, that is only 11-14x forward earnings for a Taiwan-made AIoT infrastructure supplier with a clean balance sheet and a visible dividend. In a bull case, smart-port replication, transportation/rail wins, U.S. public-safety infrastructure, and defense/drone vision push revenue above TWD 1 billion with 2023-like margins. Then EPS can move toward TWD 7-plus, and today’s price is early rather than expensive.

The defense angle should be treated as an option, not the base case. Management has explicitly pointed to military applications including drone image recognition, and the geopolitical logic is obvious: Taiwan-made, non-Chinese vision hardware plus edge AI is strategically useful. But there is not yet enough public revenue evidence to value GreenBase as a defense prime. The better thesis is that ports, rail, public safety, and industrial infrastructure fund the core earnings, while defense creates the re-rating.

The bear case is also clear. Gross margin fell from 37% to 31% in 2025, operating cash flow was negative because working capital moved against the company, and 2026 revenue is still down year to date. If April proves to be a one-month rebound and the business remains mostly customized hardware with lumpy ODM orders, the stock deserves a normal small-cap hardware multiple, not an AI multiple. Customer concentration is likely high, the Emerging Stock market is illiquid, and the first-day spike created trapped holders. The thesis is wrong if monthly revenue cannot stay above TWD 65-70 million, if gross margin stays near 31% or worse, or if announced AI/port/defense opportunities fail to become disclosed revenue.

The setup is asymmetric because expectations have reset but the optionality remains. The stock is no longer priced at the March TWD 88 enthusiasm level, yet the public narrative is still too shallow: “surveillance IPO that ran too much.” The better frame is “Taiwan-made edge vision infrastructure supplier with proven profitability, a real reference project, a parent-company ecosystem, dividend support, and a path back to TWD 5-7 EPS if vertical projects scale.”

Bear target: TWD 35, down 38%. Revenue stays near TWD 600-650 million, margin does not recover, EPS falls toward TWD 2.2-2.5, and the market assigns a 14-15x small hardware multiple with little credit for cash.

Base target: TWD 85, up 52%. Revenue reaches TWD 800-900 million over the next cycle, gross margin recovers to the mid-30s, EPS approaches TWD 4.5-5.0, and the market pays 17-19x for a profitable edge-AI infrastructure supplier with dividend support.

Bull target: TWD 130, up 132%. Smart-port, critical-infrastructure, transportation, and early defense/drone vision wins push revenue above TWD 1 billion, operating margin returns toward the 2023 level, EPS reaches about TWD 7.0, and the stock is re-rated at 18-19x as the market stops treating GreenBase as a generic camera ODM.
